title: Conversation tart
subtitle: French almond cream tart in rough puff pastry with a scored baked icing
excerpt: A French patisserie classic: a tart tin or individual tartlet shells of rough puff pastry filled with almond cream (frangipane), then topped with royal icing that bakes to a hard, crackled white surface with characteristic score marks. The name is thought to refer to the pattern of lines on the surface. Best eaten at room temperature on the day of baking.
sourceNotes: The conversation tart (tarte conversation) is a classical French patisserie preparation documented in nineteenth-century pastry texts. Jules Gouffé, Le Livre de Pâtisserie (1873, Project Gutenberg), gives a recipe for a puff pastry tart with almond filling and a latticed icing decoration consistent with the conversation tart tradition. The name is discussed in French food history writing from the same period; the precise etymology is uncertain but the recipe is consistent across sources from that time.

content.text: The defining visual element is the

attrs.term: Baked royal icing on conversation tart

content.text: scored before it goes into the oven. Make the rough puff, the frangipane, and the royal icing in sequence, then assemble and bake immediately; the icing does not improve from standing.

Rough puff: toss the cold butter cubes with the flour and salt. Add the ice cold water and mix with a knife until the dough just comes together with visible butter pieces. Flatten into a rectangle, wrap, and refrigerate for 20 minutes. Roll into a rectangle, fold into thirds (letter fold), rotate 90 degrees, and repeat three times. Refrigerate for 20 minutes after the last fold.
Frangipane: beat the butter and sugar until pale. Beat in the eggs one at a time. Fold in the almond flour, plain flour, and vanilla. The mixture should be thick and spreadable.
Royal icing: beat the egg white until frothy, then beat in the sifted icing sugar until the mixture is thick, white, and falls from the spoon in a slow ribbon.

Heat the oven to 190°C conventional (170°C fan). Roll the pastry thinly and line the tart tin; trim neatly. Spread the frangipane in an even layer across the pastry base. Spread the royal icing smoothly over the frangipane. Score diagonal lines across the icing with a knife, reaching to the pastry edge, in one direction only. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes until the icing is ivory-white and crackled and the pastry is deep golden. The icing will look wet in the first 20 minutes; it sets as the tart cools. Serve at room temperature.
